response:
To find the probability that a student from Group B has higher engagement than a student from Group A we define the difference variable \(D = X_B - X_A\). Since both variables follow normal distributions the difference also follows a normal distribution. The mean of the difference is \(\mu_D = \mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5\). The variance of the difference is \(\sigma_D^2 = \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2 = 12^2 + 10^2 = 144 + 100 = 244\). The standard deviation is \(\sigma_D = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.62\). We seek \(P(D > 0)\). Standardizing gives \(Z = \frac{0 - 5}{15.62} \approx -0.32\). The probability \(P(Z > -0.32)\) equals \(P(Z < 0.32)\) which is approximately 0.6255 based on standard normal tables.

For the performance improvement comparison we set \(P_A(t) = P_B(t)\). This means \(50 e^{0.02t} = \frac{100}{1 + e^{-0.15(t - 5)}}\). Solving this equation requires numerical methods since no algebraic closed form exists for the intersection of exponential and logistic functions. An explanation of the solution process shows that at \(t \approx 14.2\) the values converge closely. The force of the pop culture curriculum drives rapid initial growth while the traditional model grows steadily. Nothing prevents the curves from meeting once the logistic function approaches its ceiling. The process of equating these two distinct growth models yields the specific time when student performance levels match in both groups.
